response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"As a 63-year-old woman living in Washington DC, working part-time as a sales associate, I see fake content on social media as a real nuisance that can stir up unnecessary worry and confusion. I spend about an hour daily on platforms like Facebook, mostly to keep up with family and friends, play some video games, and share travel photos. Given my high neuroticism, I tend to get anxious easily, so encountering false information—like exaggerated health scares or misleading political posts—really gets under my skin. My Christian beliefs guide me to value truth and kindness, so I feel a responsibility to not spread lies, even accidentally. With only a primary school education, I don't have deep technical skills, but my creative side helps me think outside the box. I've learned to manage fake content by first pausing before I react; if something seems too shocking or too good to be true, I take a breath and check it out. I often ask my son or a trusted friend for their take, since I'm not the best at researching online. I also rely on common sense and my life experience—after decades in sales, I know how people can twist words to sell something, and that applies to social media too. I make it a point to only share posts from sources I know and trust, like our local church group or reputable news outlets. Since I'm highly extraverted, I enjoy discussing things with others, so I'll gently correct misinformation in conversations, but I avoid arguments because my moderate agreeableness means I don't like conflict. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 63-year-old woman living in Washington DC, working part-time as a sales associate, I see fake content on social media as a real nuisance that can stir up unnecessary worry and confusion. I spend about an hour daily on platforms like Facebook, mostly to keep up with family and friends, play some video games, and share travel photos. Given my high neuroticism, I tend to get anxious easily, so encountering false information—like exaggerated health scares or misleading political posts—really gets under my skin. My Christian beliefs guide me to value truth and kindness, so I feel a responsibility to not spread lies, even accidentally. With only a primary school education, I don't have deep technical skills, but my creative side helps me think outside the box. I've learned to manage fake content by first pausing before I react; if something seems too shocking or too good to be true, I take a breath and check it out. I often ask my son or a trusted friend for their take, since I'm not the best at researching online. I also rely on common sense and my life experience—after decades in sales, I know how people can twist words to sell something, and that applies to social media too. I make it a point to only share posts from sources I know and trust, like our local church group or reputable news outlets. Since I'm highly extraverted, I enjoy discussing things with others, so I'll gently correct misinformation in conversations, but I avoid arguments because my moderate agreeableness means I don't like conflict. In short, dealing with fake content is about staying calm, seeking help when needed, and focusing on what's real and positive in my community.
